channel.title: Using Data on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Using Data on emsenn.net

item.title: Aggregation and analysis
item.description:
Aggregation pipelines can amplify bias if they treat iNaturalist data as uniform. Analysis must account for uneven effort and taxonomic detection probability.

Practice guidance

  • Use spatial thinning or bias surfaces when modeling.
  • Track license and geoprivacy constraints during aggregation.
  • Document filtering and exclusion criteria.
  • Retain links to original observation media and comments for auditability.

Common aggregation pitfalls

  • Mixing data from different time windows without accounting for effort changes.
  • Treating “Research Grade” as a uniform quality filter across taxa.
  • Dropping coordinate uncertainty fields and then over-interpreting precision.

What goes wrong if you do this poorly

Aggregated datasets can create false hotspots that reflect observer behavior rather than ecological reality. Without audit trails, errors can become “institutionalized” in derived datasets.

item.title: Export and citation
item.description:
Proper export and citation preserve attribution and clarify data provenance. This matters for reproducibility and ethical reuse.

Practice guidance

  • Use iNaturalist export tools or GBIF downloads for structured data.
  • Cite both iNaturalist and the specific dataset DOI when using GBIF records.
  • Record the export date and filters used.

What goes wrong if you do this poorly

Without clear citation, downstream users cannot evaluate provenance or reproduce analyses, weakening credibility and trust.

item.title: Limits: what iNaturalist data can and cannot support
item.description:
iNaturalist data are best suited for occurrence, phenology, and broad distribution questions. They are weak for abundance, population trends, and causality without additional structure.

What iNaturalist data can support

  • Range extensions or confirmations.
  • Phenological timing of flowering, migration, or emergence.
  • Presence-only modeling with appropriate bias correction.
  • Early warning signals when paired with targeted follow-up surveys.

What iNaturalist data cannot support alone

  • Abundance or density estimates.
  • Absence-based inference without structured effort.
  • Fine-scale demographic conclusions.

Fit-for-purpose checklist

  • Is your question compatible with presence-only data?
  • Do you have a plan to model or correct sampling bias?
  • Are you combining iNaturalist with structured datasets where needed?

Strengths and pitfalls in analysis

Strengths:
